title: The role of the diaphragm in emotional regulation
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
You breathe all day and never notice 

 Your diaphragm sits there, a dome of muscle beneath your lungs, doing its job every second of every day. You don't...
htmlContent:
You breathe all day and never notice

Your diaphragm sits there, a dome of muscle beneath your lungs, doing its job every second of every day. You don't think about it. Why would you? It just moves up and down, pulling air in and pushing it out, keeping you alive. But here's the thing. That muscle isn't just a bellows for oxygen exchange. It's a direct line to your nervous system. A lever that can shift you from fight-or-flight into rest-and-digest. Or keep you locked in survival mode without you ever knowing.

I spent years not breathing properly. Not in the woo-woo, "just take a deep breath" way that people toss around like it's a cure for everything. I mean my diaphragm was tight, locked, barely moving. My chest did the work. Shallow, rapid, panicked breaths that told my brain I was in danger. And my brain believed it. Because the body doesn't lie. When your diaphragm is rigid, your vagus nerve gets the message: something is wrong. Stay alert. Don't relax.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us that the diaphragm is intimately connected to the ventral vagal pathway. That's the branch of your nervous system responsible for safety, connection, and calm. When your diaphragm moves freely and fully, it stimulates the vagus nerve. It sends a signal up to your brain saying, "We're okay. We can rest now." But when it's locked, that signal gets blocked. Your nervous system stays on high alert. And you wonder why you feel anxious all the time. Right?

Think about it. Every single emotion you've ever felt has been accompanied by a specific breathing pattern. Fear makes your breath short and high in your chest. Grief makes it catch and stutter. Anger makes it forceful and sharp. And the reverse is also true. Change your breath, and you can shift the emotion. Not perfectly. Not instantly. But the connection is there, waiting for you to use it.

What trauma does to your diaphragm

I remember the first time a bodyworker put their hands on my belly and asked me to breathe. I tried. Nothing happened. My diaphragm wouldn't drop. It was like trying to push a car with the parking brake on. She waited. I tried again. Still nothing. And then I started to shake. Not from cold. From the sheer effort of trying to do something my body had decided was unsafe.

Let me ask you something. When was the last time you felt truly relaxed in your body? Not distracted. Not numbed out. Actually relaxed. If you can't remember, that tells you something important. Your nervous system may have forgotten what rest feels like. It may have decided, somewhere along the way, that rest is dangerous.

Deb Dana talks about this - how the dorsal vagal state can look like relaxation from the outside but actually feels like collapse on the inside. There's a difference between choosing to rest and being unable to move. There's a difference between peace and shutdown. Your body knows the difference, even if the people around you can't tell.

Notice your hands right now. Are they clenched? Open? Cold? Warm? Your hands tell a story about your autonomic state. Cold hands often mean sympathetic activation - blood has moved to the core, preparing for threat. Warm, relaxed hands suggest ventral vagal engagement - safety. It's a small thing to notice. But small things are where healing lives.

Trauma lives in the body. That's not a metaphor. Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, has spent decades documenting how unprocessed survival responses get trapped in our physical structures. Your diaphragm is one of the primary places this happens. When you're in danger, your body prepares for action. Your breath quickens. Your diaphragm tenses to brace for impact. That's normal. But if the threat never resolves, if you never get to complete the fight-or-flight response, that tension stays. It becomes chronic. Your diaphragm forgets how to relax.

And here's what nobody tells you. This isn't just about anxiety or panic attacks. A locked diaphragm affects everything. Your digestion slows down because your stomach and intestines don't get the gentle massage they need from your breathing. Your posture collapses. Your neck and shoulders take on the work your diaphragm won't do. You get headaches, jaw tension, back pain. You feel exhausted because your body is working overtime just to get enough air. And you blame yourself for being lazy or weak. But it's not your fault. It's your nervous system trying to protect you, long after the danger is gone.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Levine walks through how the body holds trauma and what we can do about it. It's not a quick fix. But it's honest about how this work actually feels.

The diaphragm and the vagus nerve are best friends

Your vagus nerve runs from your brainstem all the way down to your abdomen, passing right through the diaphragm. It's like a superhighway of information between your brain and your organs. And the diaphragm is a major exit ramp. Every time you inhale fully, your diaphragm drops down and stimulates the vagus nerve. Every time you exhale slowly, the vagus nerve gets a gentle massage. This is why extended exhales are so calming. They're directly activating your parasympathetic nervous system.

But most of us are doing the opposite. We're taking short, incomplete inhales and even shorter exhales. We're cutting the vagus nerve off from its natural stimulation. And then we wonder why we can't calm down. Stay with me here. This is not about forcing yourself to breathe in some unnatural pattern for hours a day. It's about undoing the tension that's been there for years. It's about giving your diaphragm permission to move the way it was designed to.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist and author, talks about how the diaphragm can become "frozen" in a state of high alert. She describes it as a kind of muscular armoring, a term that goes back to Wilhelm Reich but has real validity when we look at it through a nervous system lens. The body braces against feeling. And the diaphragm, being central to both breathing and emotional expression, is ground zero for that bracing. You can't feel grief if your diaphragm won't let you sob. You can't feel anger if your diaphragm won't let you roar. You can't feel joy if your diaphragm won't let you laugh fully. The emotion gets stuck. And so does the breath.

A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). Sometimes the nervous system needs a break from constant sensory input to let the body settle. These help.

Practical ways to work with your diaphragm

I'm not going to tell you to take ten deep breaths and call it a day. That's insulting to anyone who's actually struggling. But I will tell you what has helped me and the people I work with. Start by noticing. Put one hand on your belly and one hand on your chest. Breathe normally. Which hand moves more? If it's the one on your chest, your diaphragm is probably not moving much. That's okay. You're just gathering information.

Your body remembers what your mind has forgotten. That's not poetry - that's neuroscience. Peter Levine spent decades documenting how trauma lives in the tissues, in the fascia, in the way your muscles hold tension patterns that mirror the exact posture you were in when the worst thing happened.

I've seen this in my own body. The way my shoulders round forward when I feel threatened - that's the fetal position, compressed into a standing posture. The way my jaw clenches before I even know I'm stressed - that's years of swallowed words, swallowed screams, swallowed truth. Your body is an archive. It stores everything.

Here's what I want you to try. Put one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Don't try to change anything. Just notice. Is your breath high and shallow? Is your belly tight? Are your shoulders up by your ears? Whatever you find, it's okay. You're not doing it wrong. You're just meeting yourself where you are. That's the beginning of everything.

Now try something simple. Lie on your back with your knees bent. Put a book or a small pillow on your belly. Breathe and try to make the book rise on your inhale. That's it. Just let the breath drop down into your belly. If nothing happens, don't force it. Your diaphragm might not be ready to release. That's a sign of how much tension is there, not a failure on your part. Be patient. This takes time.

Another thing that helps is sound. Humming, sighing, and chanting all vibrate the vagus nerve and can help the diaphragm relax. I hum when I'm driving. I sigh when I realize I've been holding my breath. I make weird noises in the shower. It feels silly at first. But it works. The vibration travels through your chest and belly and tells your nervous system, "We're safe enough to make noise. We're safe enough to be heard."

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). It gets into the mechanics of how the body completes trauma responses, including the role of breath and movement.

What it feels like when the diaphragm starts to open

I want to be honest with you about this. Releasing a locked diaphragm is not always pleasant. Sometimes it brings up old feelings that were held in that tension. You might feel sad. You might feel angry. You might feel nothing at all, which can be its own kind of unsettling. That's normal. The body is letting go of something it's been holding for a long time. It doesn't always happen gracefully.

But there's also this. When your diaphragm starts to move freely, you notice things you didn't notice before. You feel more present in your body. Your voice might change, get fuller, more resonant. Your digestion improves. Your sleep gets deeper. You find yourself sighing less because you're not holding your breath as much. And the anxiety, the background hum of threat that's been there for as long as you can remember, it gets quieter. Not gone. But quieter. More manageable. You start to trust that you can regulate yourself, even when things get hard.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk lays out the science in a way that makes sense of what you've been feeling.

Your diaphragm is not just a muscle. It's a messenger. It's telling your nervous system whether you're safe or in danger, whether you can rest or need to fight. And the good news is, you can change that message. Not by forcing it. Not by pushing through. But by listening. By giving it space to move again. By breathing in a way that says, "I'm here. I'm safe. I can let go."

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that last sentence. Did your breath change? Did your shoulders drop? Did you feel a small release somewhere? That's your diaphragm talking. Keep listening.

Healing Journey Resources

  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Peter Levine's exploration of body-based trauma healing
  • Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link) - For reducing sensory overload and supporting nervous system regulation
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - Peter Levine's guide to somatic experiencing and trauma completion
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's look at trauma and the body
